nodes.description: The frontend is a single Next.js workspace that concentrates the main product behaviors: project selection, note canvas, conversations, composer actions, and git controls. `Home` in `frontend/app/page.tsx` owns the bulk of client state, toggles between Notes and Project views, and coordinates build, plan, ask, commit, push, and canvas-edit flows through the API layer. This is the primary user surface where architecture mapping and implementation work meet.
nodes.title: Workspace Shell

nodes.description: The visual canvas is the direct manipulation surface for workspace notes. `CanvasBoard` renders note cards and relationship edges with React Flow, supports viewport persistence, selection, dragging, context menus, and exploratory branches, and shows only the edges relevant to the current focus. It is the main spatial interface for understanding architecture rather than a passive diagram renderer.
nodes.title: Canvas Board

nodes.description: Konceptura keeps a lightweight project profile with the active repository and recent repositories so the workspace can reopen known codebases quickly. `ProjectService` updates the profile, lists project tree items, computes workspace status such as visible file count and existing canvas notes, and creates `.konceptura` metadata storage inside the repo when needed. This gives the product its local-first project switching behavior.
nodes.title: Project Registry

nodes.description: Project conversations are persisted per repository so planning and build sessions can continue over time instead of acting like stateless prompts. `ProjectService` stores and orders conversation records in `.konceptura/conversations.json`, exposes placeholder conversations for untouched repos, and supports read, create, and update endpoints. In the UI, these conversations feed the console and become reusable context for Codex runs.
nodes.title: Conversation Memory

nodes.description: The workspace includes commit and push operations as first-class product actions so code changes can move from local edits to versioned output without leaving the app. `ProjectService` detects git roots, computes changed files and branch state, performs `git add`, `git commit`, and `git push`, while `CodexService` can suggest a conventional commit message from diff context. The frontend surfaces this as actionable controls tied to the currently open repo.
nodes.title: Git Publishing

nodes.description: Indexing turns a repository into the graph substrate that powers search, structure browsing, and impact hints. `IndexService` launches the local `code-graph-rag` binary, tracks a single running job in `state.json`, supports dry-run command previews, and marks the active repository only after a successful run. This is the gateway from raw source tree to explorable graph-backed workspace.
nodes.title: Repository Indexing

nodes.description: The graph layer exposes symbols, relationships, freeform Cypher queries, and a nested project structure built from Memgraph data. `GraphService` wraps Neo4j-compatible queries against Memgraph, ranks symbol matches heuristically, resolves symbol paths, and reconstructs the structure tree from containment and definition edges. This is the factual code graph boundary the rest of the product leans on.
nodes.title: Graph Explorer

nodes.description: Before asking Codex to change code, Konceptura can derive likely seeds, related symbols, and affected files from the graph. `GraphService.analyze_impact` combines ranked symbol matching with nearby `CALLS` and `IMPORTS` relationships to produce a practical file shortlist and human-readable reasons. This acts as a lightweight change-planning policy rather than a full semantic planner.
nodes.title: Impact Analysis

nodes.description: All LLM-assisted behavior is centralized in `CodexService`, which builds Codex CLI commands, merges semantic and conversation context, optionally injects graph-derived hints, and snapshots repositories to compute changed files. It supports direct code changes, planning, repo Q&A, commit message generation, exploration suggestions, architecture-note generation, and note-edit drafting. This is the automation boundary between the workspace and the local Codex CLI.
nodes.title: Codex Execution Engine

nodes.description: The backend exposes a unified run workflow for ask, plan, build, and auto mode so the frontend can treat assisted work as a progressive session instead of isolated RPC calls. `/project/run-stream` emits NDJSON phases and final payloads, while `/project/build`, `/project/plan`, and `/project/ask` provide direct request-response variants. In build mode, this orchestration combines code edits with architecture-note refresh in one operation.
nodes.title: Run Orchestrator

nodes.description: Architecture notes are persisted per repository inside `.konceptura/canvas.json`, making the visual model part of the working project state rather than a transient frontend artifact. `CanvasService` handles CRUD for nodes and edges, deduplicates generated note titles, computes note-change summaries, and places generated nodes into tag-based zones with collision avoidance. This is the persistent knowledge layer behind the canvas UI.
nodes.title: Canvas Note Store

nodes.description: Canvas edits can be proposed by Codex and selectively applied, which turns note maintenance into a reviewable workflow instead of blind regeneration. `preview_canvas_edits` finds directly targeted and impacted notes, `CodexService.draft_canvas_changes` proposes structured create/update/delete/link operations, and `CanvasService.apply_canvas_edit_changes` resolves dependencies and applies only accepted changes. This is the product’s editorial control layer for architecture notes.
nodes.title: Canvas Review Workflow

nodes.description: The app is explicitly self-hosted and local-first: configuration points to local Memgraph, local `code-graph-rag`, a locally discovered Codex binary, backend state files, and sample repositories. `Settings` loads these values from `konceptura.toml` and environment variables, and the README describes the expected dev stack around ports 3001 and 8000. This boundary explains why indexing, graph queries, and code changes are all treated as local machine capabilities.
nodes.title: Local Tooling Boundary
